figures.caption: Table 2: SPOT-Bench Task Taxonomy. We report dataset scale (#Videos) and per-video statistics reflecting streaming density for each task in SPOT-Bench. Responses denote avg. responses per turn averaged across all videos. MT/MR indicates multi-turn and multiresponse respectively.
Table 5: Evaluation of offline MLLMs on SPOT-Bench using Timeliness score (K=1) (↑).
Figure 9: An illustrated example of the Action Boundary Detection (ABD) task. The highlighted “same” is referential, i.e. it points back to the skillet mentioned in the first query.
Figure 10: Illustrated examples of the Point-of-No-Return Detection (PNR) task. The top example shows a multi-turn, single-response case; the bottom example shows a single-turn, multi-response case.

Figure 12: An illustrated example of the Streaming Procedural Guidance (SPG) task. A set of allowed action labels is given upfront, and the model must respond with the correct next action at the correct times as the video streams.

Figure 13: An illustrated example of the Solicited Intervention (SI) task. The user provides a task description and a set of allowed actions, and the model issues corrective guidance only when it detects an error in the streaming video.
Figure 11: An illustrated example of the Streaming Question Answering (SQA) task. The gap between ask time and response time is typically larger for this task.

Figure 2: Overview of SPOT-Bench tasks. Each example illustrates proactive queries expecting streaming predictions over time with red boxes marking keyframes. Tasks are grouped into detection, intervention, and interaction benchmarks.

Table 3: Comparison of Online VideoQA benchmarks. Benchmarks are sorted in ascending order of %Proactive. Symbols: ✓ supported, ✗ not supported. ConcurrentQA, Unsolicited and Streaming Evaluation are unique to SPOT-Bench.
Table 4: Evaluation of streaming models on SPOT-Bench. Each cell reports Timelinessscore@5 / Timeliness-F1@5. All models share the same Qwen2.5-VL [8] backbone.

Figure 8: Human Preference Intervals. Gold intervals derived from our controlled human study with 10 MTurk annotators. Blue dots show annotator response times relative to 0s (Sec. 4.3). For detection tasks (PNR and ABD), gray dots mark the precise keyframe timestamps provided by the source datasets.
Figure 7: Additional SPOT-Bench Statistics. Overview of video durations, turn counts and our special turn types: Referential and Concurrent.

Figure 14: Illustrated examples of the Unsolicited Intervention (UI) task. The top example shows a multi-response scenario, where the model issues several warnings as new hazards appear; the bottom example shows a single-response case.

Table 1: Evaluation on prior Online VideoQA benchmarks. We report results on StreamingBench [37] and OVO-Bench [42] for closed-source and open-source MLLMs. 0 frames denotes blind baseline (no visual input); 1 frame uses only the query frame; 4 frames denote the 4 most recent frames at 1fps.

Figure 6: Our annotation pipeline. We first define a task taxonomy and probe curated datasets for relevant videos. After filtering according to the taxonomy, we generate groundtruth annotations in a semi-automatic process where LLMs assist human annotators.
